plant_parts.quote: Symptoms of BPMV are green to yellow mottling (blotchiness) of younger leaves in the upper canopy. Infected plants may have green stems after the pods have matured. Seed from infected plants may appear smoky in color or have reduced size and quality due to seed coat mottling which will be brown or black depending on hilum color.

plant_parts.quote: On tomatoes infected with TSWV, the uppermost leaves appear bronzed with small brown lesions (Figure 1). Infected peppers form small brown necrotic spots and/or chlorotic ring spots on the foliage (Figure 2). Infected foliage on both tomatoes and peppers may become covered with so many spots that the entire leaf browns, rolls, and wilts. Infected seedlings form a "shepherd's crook" appearance and die (Figure 3). Early infection results in total loss of fruit. Established plants that become infected are stunted with decreased yields. Symptoms on the fruit of both tomatoes and peppers typically include yellow ringspots and brown necrotic sunken areas (Figures 4 and 5).

notes.value: Symptoms begin as small, light green spots without water-soaking, with light-colored raised blisters (pustules) developing in the center of lesions on both upper and lower leaf surfaces; lesions can merge into large irregular patches of dead tissue and small raised spots may also develop on pods.
notes.quote: Symptoms begin as small, light green spots (without water-soaking). Light-colored, raised blisters (pustules) often develop in the center of lesions on both the upper and lower surface of leaves. The lesions can grow together into large, irregular patches of dead tissue. Loss of tissue in the infected areas may give the leaves a ragged appearance like bacterial blight. Small raised spots may also develop on pods.

distinctive_signs: Under a hand lens, the spore-bearing structures resemble bunches of grapes; small green fruit infected directly by airborne spores develop white, circular rings called 'ghost spots'; infected fruit in storage shows the typical gray coating of spores and may have a white mycelium on the surface.
visual.notes: Gray-brown velvety mold covers stems or leaves of young plants; gray spores cover dying flowers and the calyx of fruit, with stem cankers that may girdle and cause wilting above the infected area.

plant_parts.quote: The disease can cause spotting and decay of flowers, leaves, fruits, and berries. On some plants, such as roses, it can cause slightly sunken areas called cankers on the stems. Corms and bulbs of perennials and annuals may rot when infected with gray mold.

distinctive_signs.quote: Under a hand lens, the spore-bearing structures resemble bunches of grapes... Small green fruit infected directly by airborne spores instead of by contact with other infections usually does not decay, but develops white, circular rings called "'ghost spots."' Infected fruit held in storage at high humidity often decays and shows the typical gray coating of spores; it may also have a white mycelium on the surface.

notes.quote: Gray mold appears on young plants as gray-brown velvety mold covering stems or leaves. Infections that girdle the stem cause wilting above the infected area. In the field gray spores cover dying flowers and the calyx of fruit.

color.quote: The lesion shows a central white-to-grayish fungal mass surrounded by tan/beige necrotic tissue on an otherwise pale green unripe tomato fruit.

shape.value: roughly circular sunken lesion with radial cracks
shape.quote: A roughly circular sunken lesion sits at the stem-end of the fruit with star-like radial cracks splitting outward through the surrounding tan tissue.

margin.quote: The lesion margin is irregular and jagged where the tan necrotic tissue meets the surrounding pale green fruit surface, with no sharp halo.

texture.quote: The center shows fluffy white mycelial growth within a sunken, collapsed area, while the surrounding necrotic tissue is dry, papery, and cracked.

distinctive_signs: Tomato plants infected by the gray mold fungus have light tan or gray spots that are covered with a brown mold on the upper leaf surface.

distinctive_signs: White, cottony mycelium that produces large, irregularly shaped, black sclerotia; bleached appearance of dried killed stems; stem lesions show hollow pith when cut open with white moldy growth at the edges and small black sclerotia in or on the lesion.
visual.notes: White mold appears at flowering as water-soaked areas on flowers and stem joints; infected stems are killed, dry out and take on a bleached appearance, and affected areas show white, cottony mycelium with large black sclerotia. Infected fruits turn gray and rot.

plant_parts.quote: Symptoms include water-soaked areas on flowers and at stem joints where senescent flower petals have fallen. The infection quickly kills stems, which eventually dry and take on a bleached appearance. Water-soaked stem lesions may also appear at the soil line ... Infected fruits turn gray and rot.

distinctive_signs.value: White, cottony mycelium that produces large, irregularly shaped, black sclerotia; bleached appearance of dried killed stems; stem lesions show hollow pith when cut open with white moldy growth at the edges and small black sclerotia in or on the lesion.
distinctive_signs.quote: Lesions on the stem show hollow pith when cut open, and white moldy growth is usually visible at the edges of the lesions. Small black spherical fruiting bodies (sclerotia) are usually observed in or on the lesion.
